posts.title: SAP S/4HANA 2025: What You Need to Know Before You Migrate
posts.description: SAP has set a firm deadline for ECC support. Here is what enterprise IT leaders must assess before committing to a migration path.
posts.author: Proximsoft Team
posts.body:
SAP's end-of-mainstream-maintenance deadline for ECC is 2027, and organisations that delay their S/4HANA journey risk being left on an unsupported platform — exposed to security vulnerabilities and cut off from innovation. Yet rushing into a migration without a clear strategy is equally dangerous.

Assess Your Current Landscape First

Before committing to a migration path — greenfield, brownfield, or selective data transition — enterprise IT leaders need a complete picture of their existing ECC environment. This means cataloguing custom ABAP code, identifying third-party integrations, and understanding which business processes are tightly coupled to legacy configurations.

A system landscape assessment typically uncovers thousands of custom objects, many of which are either duplicative or no longer in use. Cleaning this up before migration dramatically reduces risk and effort.

Choose the Right Migration Approach

The three primary pathways each carry different tradeoffs:

  • Greenfield (new implementation): Start fresh on S/4HANA with clean processes. Maximum opportunity to adopt best practices, but highest change management burden.
  • Brownfield (system conversion): Convert your existing ECC system in place. Faster and lower disruption, but you carry forward technical debt.
  • Selective data transition: Merge or consolidate multiple legacy systems into a single S/4HANA instance. Complex, but often the right choice for post-merger integration scenarios.

Plan for Data Migration Early

Data quality is consistently the top cause of S/4HANA project delays. The new universal journal (ACDOCA) in S/4HANA requires clean, reconciled financial data before go-live. Begin data cleansing activities in parallel with your design phase — not at the end of the project.

Build a Realistic Business Case

S/4HANA migration is a significant investment. A credible business case should quantify both hard savings (licence consolidation, infrastructure reduction, process automation) and strategic benefits (real-time reporting, AI readiness, faster close cycles). Align your CFO and business sponsors before technical scoping begins.

Proximsoft has delivered S/4HANA migrations across manufacturing, logistics, and financial services sectors. If you are at the assessment stage, contact our SAP practice to discuss your landscape and timelines.

posts.title: Salesforce Einstein AI: Turning CRM Data Into Revenue
posts.description: How enterprise sales and service teams are using Salesforce Einstein to surface the right insights at exactly the right moment in the customer journey.
posts.author: Proximsoft Team
posts.body:
Most enterprises are sitting on years of untapped CRM data — activity logs, opportunity histories, support cases, email interactions. Salesforce Einstein AI transforms that data into revenue-generating intelligence, surfacing the right action for the right rep at the right moment in the sales cycle.

Lead Scoring That Actually Works

Einstein Lead Scoring uses machine learning to analyse historical conversion patterns and rank incoming leads by their likelihood to close. Unlike traditional rule-based scoring models built by administrators, Einstein continuously retrains on your own data — so the model improves as your business evolves.

Sales teams using Einstein Lead Scoring typically report a 20–35% improvement in rep productivity by focusing effort on leads most likely to convert rather than working through lists chronologically.

Opportunity Insights and Deal Health

Einstein Opportunity Scoring gives sales managers a real-time view of deal health across the entire pipeline. Deals showing signs of stall — no recent activity, missing stakeholders, slipping close dates — are flagged automatically. Managers can intervene early rather than discovering lost deals at month-end.

Service Intelligence with Einstein Case Classification

On the service side, Einstein Case Classification reads incoming support cases and automatically routes them to the right team, sets priority, and suggests resolution paths based on similar historical cases. This reduces average handle time and improves first-contact resolution rates.

Getting Started: Data Quality is the Foundation

Einstein is only as good as the data it trains on. Before deploying any Einstein feature, assess your CRM data completeness — are opportunity fields populated consistently? Are contacts linked to accounts? Are activities logged reliably?

Proximsoft's Salesforce practice helps enterprises prepare their data foundation and deploy Einstein features in a phased rollout that drives measurable business outcomes from day one. Speak to our team about an Einstein readiness assessment.

posts.title: Building an AI-Ready Data Platform: A Practical Roadmap
posts.description: Most enterprises have the data — but not the architecture. Here is how to move from fragmented data silos to a modern, AI-ready data platform.
posts.author: Proximsoft Team
posts.body:
The difference between enterprises that successfully deploy AI and those that do not is rarely the algorithm — it is the data architecture underneath. AI models are only as reliable as the data they consume, and most enterprise data landscapes were built for reporting, not for machine learning.

Audit Your Current Data Landscape

Start with a data estate inventory. Map every significant data source in your organisation: ERP systems, CRM platforms, data warehouses, flat files, third-party feeds. Identify where data is duplicated, where definitions conflict (is 'revenue' the same in finance and sales?), and where critical data gaps exist.

This audit typically reveals three categories of data assets: clean and trusted, partially reliable, and unreliable or unknown. Only clean and trusted data should feed AI models in production.

Establish a Unified Data Layer

A modern data platform consolidates disparate sources into a single governed layer. The most common architectural patterns today are:

  • Lakehouse: Combines the scale of a data lake with the governance of a data warehouse. Platforms like Databricks and Microsoft Fabric are leading this space.
  • Cloud Data Warehouse: Snowflake, BigQuery, and Redshift remain excellent choices for structured analytical workloads.
  • Data Mesh: A decentralised approach where domain teams own their own data products. Appropriate for large organisations with mature data cultures.

Implement a Data Governance Framework

AI amplifies data quality problems — a biased or incomplete training dataset produces a biased or unreliable model. Before training any model on enterprise data, put governance controls in place: data cataloguing, lineage tracking, quality scoring, and access controls.

Start with High-Value, Bounded AI Use Cases

Resist the temptation to boil the ocean. The highest-ROI initial AI deployments are typically bounded, well-defined use cases with clear success metrics: demand forecasting, predictive maintenance, invoice classification, or customer churn prediction.

Choose a use case where you already have 18–24 months of clean historical data, a business owner who understands the problem deeply, and a clear definition of what 'better' looks like.

Build for Reuse, Not One-Off Models

The goal of an AI-ready data platform is not a single model — it is an infrastructure that lets your organisation launch, monitor, and iterate on AI capabilities at scale. Invest in MLOps tooling (model registries, experiment tracking, deployment pipelines) from the start, not as an afterthought.

Proximsoft's data and AI practice works with enterprises at every stage of this journey — from initial landscape assessment to full production deployment. Talk to our data team about your AI readiness.
